title: How to Work with Career Dissatisfaction That Surfaces During Journeys
body:
How to Work with Career Dissatisfaction That Surfaces During Journeys

You're on a psychedelic journey. Maybe it’s your first time or your fifteenth. Suddenly, the familiar feeling bubbles up - career dissatisfaction. That gnawing sense that your job, your path, or your daily grind isn’t what you thought it would be. It hits you with clarity. You think, “Why am I doing this? Am I stuck? Should I quit? What next?”

Right?! That moment can feel like a punch in the gut. Psychedelics have a way of shining a brutal spotlight on the parts of your life that need attention. And career dissatisfaction is one of those areas that loves to sneak out from the shadows during these experiences. But here’s the thing, you’re not alone in this. It’s common. It’s raw. And it’s okay.

Why Does Career Dissatisfaction Surface During Psychedelic Journeys?

Truth is, psychedelics reduce the noise your brain usually makes. They lower your cognitive defenses. That’s why when you’re under their influence, your subconscious can bring forward emotions, memories, and thoughts that you usually keep locked away. Career dissatisfaction often sits there, simmering, under layers of busyness and distraction.

When those layers peel back, what’s left is this stark, sometimes uncomfortable truth: you’re not happy where you are. Maybe you’re in a job that drains you, or a career path that feels misaligned with your values. This revelation can feel like a crisis or an opportunity, depending on how you approach it.

Consider this: a friend of mine, Sarah, once told me after her journey, “I realized I’d been climbing a ladder leaning against the wrong building for years.” She didn’t get immediate answers on the trip, but the dissatisfaction was clear as day. That clarity was her starting point.

Step 1: Don’t Run From the Feeling

When career dissatisfaction shows up, the first impulse is to push it away. To pretend it’s not there or to distract yourself with the usual coping mechanisms - work harder, scroll your phone, binge-watch shows. But that’s exactly what you don’t want to do.

Look, it’s uncomfortable. There’s no sugarcoating that. But leaning into the discomfort opens a door. You can’t fix what you don’t accept. So, when you’re coming down from a journey and that feeling is loud, sit with it. Let it be as messy or as clear as it wants to be.

Here’s a practical move: write down exactly what you’re feeling. Don’t edit or censor. Just dump it all on paper or into your phone. It could be as simple as, “I hate my job,” or as complex as, “I’m scared I don’t have the skills to change.” Whatever it is, name it.

Step 2: Separate the Feeling From the Story

Know what I mean? There’s a difference between the raw feeling of dissatisfaction and the stories your mind builds around it. Stories like “I’m a failure,” or “I’m too old to switch careers,” or “I’ll never be financially stable if I leave.” Those stories aren’t facts. They’re stories. And stories change.

Try this exercise: ask yourself, “What’s the feeling beneath the story?” For example, behind “I’m a failure” might be fear or shame. Behind “I’m too old” might be anxiety about change or judgment. Recognizing feelings separate from stories can help you avoid getting stuck in your own mental trap.

If you’re into reading, The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) offers excellent insight into how emotions are stored and how you can begin to work with them without getting tangled in self-criticism.

Step 3: Use Small, Concrete Steps to Explore Change

Here’s the thing, big career changes don’t happen overnight. It’s rarely about quitting your job the day after your trip. Instead, start with small, manageable steps that help you explore what you want instead of what you don’t.

  • Make a list of things you love about your current job, no matter how small.
  • Make another list of things that make you feel drained or unhappy.
  • Brainstorm one or two small things you could do this week to bring more of what you love into your life or reduce what you hate.

For example, maybe you realize you love creative problem-solving but hate endless meetings. Your small step could be asking your manager if you can take lead on a new project that taps into that creativity. Or maybe it’s carving out 30 minutes a day to work on a passion project outside of work.

If you want to measure your microdosing doses precisely to keep your journeys safe and consistent while exploring these feelings, consider getting a precision milligram scale (paid link). It’s a shift for anyone serious about dosing.

Step 4: Set Boundaries to Protect Your Energy

Dissatisfaction often comes with exhaustion. Maybe you’ve been giving all your energy to a job that doesn’t give back. It’s okay to protect what you have left. Saying “no” isn’t selfish - it’s necessary.

Maybe it’s turning off work emails after 6 PM. Maybe it’s scheduling “do nothing” time during the weekend. Or maybe it’s setting limits on how much you talk about work at home. Boundaries help you hold space for new possibilities to grow.

One of the easiest ways to support your nervous system during this process is getting good rest. A silk sleep eye mask (paid link) can help block out distractions and improve sleep quality. Trust me, better sleep changes everything.

Step 5: Seek Support Without Spiritual Bypassing

Look, this is where things get tricky. You might want to talk to friends, therapists, or coaches. That’s great. But be mindful of spiritual bypassing. That’s when someone tells you to “just let it go” or “trust the process” without acknowledging the real pain and complexity beneath.

Real support means holding space for your doubts, fears, and anger. It means a therapist or friend saying, “Yeah, that sucks. Let’s figure this out together.”

If you want a well-researched, scientific overview of how psychedelics affect mental health and how that relates to career stress, check out this article from Nature. It’s dense but worth it.

Also, I’ve written more about working with inner conflict during journeys, which ties directly into handling these kinds of difficult feelings without running away.

Step 6: Give Yourself Time

Honestly, change takes time. Your brain, body, and emotions all need space to adjust. That means don’t expect your career dissatisfaction to flip into career clarity overnight. It’s more like peeling back one layer of an onion every week, sometimes tearing up a little, sometimes laughing.

It’s okay to pause and just be. Sometimes the best action is rest. Other times it’s talking it out. Other times it’s applying for a new job or signing up for a class. You get to find your own rhythm. No one else’s.

Final Thoughts: Remember You’re Not Broken

Here’s the truth: feeling dissatisfied with your career is not a personal failure. It doesn’t mean you’re lazy, unworthy, or lost forever. It means you’re human. And it means you’re growing. The psychedelic journey just happens to be the mirror showing you where you might need to make a change.

If you want to keep learning about conscious healing and how to sit with these feelings, visit my site at kalesh.love. You’ll find tools, stories, and resources to help you along the way.

“Your work is to discover your work and then with all your heart to give yourself to it.” - Buddha (but, hey, this doesn’t mean rush. Take your time.)

Remember, you’re not alone in this. Hold your discomfort like a fragile bird. Watch it. Learn from it. And move forward - one small step at a time.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
